author: tersec
content:
question, suppose there was a fork a couple epochs prior and some shuffling changes since so two proposer share a slot. how does FOCIL's 2 slot retention work here? to apply each block still requires an IL. so is it that those ILs not on what the client considers a canonical chain must be checked but not kept, and those on the client's canonical chain (which is the usual req/resp criteria) have to be kept?

and if a client reorgs, see above, if it hasn't been keeping the ILs on other forks, it would mean that at least for 1 slot it couldn't return 2 slots retention

author: jih2nn
content:
IIUC, the question is about when a node switches to a different branch.

1.
When a node moves to a different branch, it will follow what attesters on that branch have decided. You don't need ILs to process each block. ILs are for fork-choice and it is decided by those attesters on the branch. However, you need to know whether the payload at the tip satisfied the ILs. This is to ensure you will have the same fork-choice view after moving to another branch. This is why the EL needs to remember ILs for the tip of each branch.

2.
IL committee is a subset of beacon committee. As long as it hasn't passed 2 epochs since the fork happened, branches will share IL committees, so you will have 2 slots retention for different branches. If the divergence lasts more than 2 epochs, you will have ILs only for your branch. This can lead to a different IL-view after you move to a different branch. This is why I think we should turn-off IL enforcement under non-finalizing situation. The network will be likely converge, but prioritizing the network recovery in such case makes more sense IMO.

3.
A node from a different branch can produce an IL that might contain some transaction that cannot be included in your branch, but this is fine. All nodes on your branch will use the same state, so it will lead to the same result deterministically.
